sentences.text: [SIGHS] We manage to get ourselves up, separately.
And there we were, in this apartment.
And it was like heaven, of course, let alone-- let alone the comfort, let alone the warmth, let alone everything else.
But we were in heaven.
We were-- it was a haven and a heaven for us.
And I couldn't speak one word of German in those days.
Whatever she said to me, I-- I said, ja, just in case, because I thought maybe it's better than to say, nein.
And my mother spoke very fluently in German.
And immediately, my mother sat down to do some sewing for her.
And she was in seventh heaven.
Because that's all she ever wanted.
With my mother, she-- she wanted to monopolize her, which she very barely could do at work.
But here, she-- she-- she was absolutely thrilled.
So, naturally, everything by hand, because there was no machine, number one, and second, even if-- if there were, you couldn't possibly make any noise using it anyway.
And she went out to work during the day.
And we-- we would not be allowed to move at all, because of-- heaven help us, anybody should hear anything from upstairs.
So you can't imagine.
It's-- it's all very well.
But if one needs to, for instance, go to the toilet, and you can't, or, if you go to there, and you can't flush the water or, you know, all these things.
If you want to have a drink, you cannot open the faucet, because, heaven help us, somebody will hear.
[SIGHS] It was pretty tough, difficult from that point of view.
But no-- we were not complaining, because we were in such-- in such wonderful, you know, surroundings.
I mean, who had that.
Everything.
I didn't feel like a 12-year-old.
I felt like a 25-year-old or-- or more, you know?
There was no question of-- of feeling one's age as a child.
I mean, I can't imagine other children felt differently.
We were all very grown-up.
And-- and I-- I felt wonderful.
But, of course, I knew that it's not lasting.
It's only for a few days.
And we were all worrying, how shall we get out of here?
What shall we do?
So we-- how shall we get out of here?
Now there is a question that one requires, as I said, these patches, either W, for Wehrmacht, or R, for [GERMAN].
Where can we get it?
How can we get it?
Huh?
We're stuck here now.
And there's no way of--
But Frau Wieth, we knew, had a Jewish doctor, whom she attended, had a Jewish chemist.
Because she believed that only they are the best, and she wouldn't go to anybody else.
So long as they were allowed to be outside and practice or even, you know, they were a labor force, but that they were.
And we knew that this doctor, she goes to him quite often and so on.
And my mother had the idea to ask.
She said, would you go to Dr.
Schwieger, ?] when-- for an examination, whatever, while you are there, would you ask him to provide, for you, a patch, an R or a W?
I'll pay for it.
One could buy things.
You know, everything was on black market.
I mean, it wasn't anything-- there wasn't there wasn't anything you could not buy if you had the money.
We didn't.
We never had any money.
But for that, one would have had to.
And she said, would you tell him that you'll buy it, whatever it costs.
So she went.
And she went, and when she asked him, I think like he suddenly stood absolutely amazed.
He said, why-- why do you need a thing like this?
What do you want that for?
It's obvious that he was surprised, right?
[DRINKS] She said, because I have with me a Jewish lady, with a little girl, with her daughter.
And they're going back to the ghetto.
That is to say, the Jewish lady will go back to the ghetto.
Because I don't want the girl to go back.
And he said, what do you mean?
How come that they are with you?
And he couldn't comprehend at all.
It was an-- an admission, you know, of-- she was foolish to do.
But there was no way of-- of procuring that sort of thing otherwise.
Where from?
So he said, what do you mean, you are going to keep this girl?
She said, yes.
I'm not going to give her to the Gestapo, oh, no, no.
Not this girl, I'm keeping her.
If her mother wants to go back, it's her business.
So she told him.
He said, all right, I-- I'll get you a patch.
How do you know all of this?
Because my-- how-- she-- I'll tell you.
Right.
She came home.
And she said, it's all right, Frau Stern, to my mother.
It's all right.
I saw Dr.
Schwieger. ?] He will get you an R or a W.
Oh, wonderful.
My mother says, thank you.
This is wonderful, wonderful, because, you see, she had her parents in the ghetto.
And we didn't know, were they dead, alive.
We didn't know anything.
We had no communication.
And the selection was going on.
She definitely wanted to go back.
We wanted to go back.
We didn't want to stay.
And so my mother said, so, how-- how did it go?
How did you manage to tell him?
It's-- you know, it's very dangerous, what you are doing.
She said, yes, I told him.
I told him.
I have a Jewish lady with her little girl here.
And-- and-- and that you want to go back to the ghetto.
Then all right.
And you can go back to the ghetto if you want to.
But I'm not giving you Lili.
I want you to know that.
My mother's like-- And she said, what do you mean?
You-- you can't keep her.
Well, she said, yes, I can.
I'm going to keep her.
If anything happens to you, don't worry, I'll adopt her.
I didn't speak one word of German, nothing.
It was so uncanny.
It was unbelievable.
And she said, in two days, I'm going to collect the patch.
And you think about it.
Because you don't have to go back.
You can stay here, too.
She said-- my mother said, no, no, no, we must go back, because my parents are there.
No.
Yeah, well, as you said-- as I said, but I am not going to give you Lili.
And I told Dr.
Schwieger. ?]
She said, why did you tell him things like this?
Oh, I told him, because it's true.
Because she was such a very naive and very straightforward person.
And sometimes, she would say silly things, because she thought.
That's what she thought.
She said whatever was on her-- on her mind was on her tongue.
Which was not the right thing to do in those days.
She went to get the patch.
He had it prepared for her.
I don't know how much or how little.
I can't tell you now.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
And she said-- and he said to her, look, we have no children, as you know, but I have a niece.
She's 17 years old.
I wonder, would you take her?
And for that, I will pay you.
And he started telling her how much, in dollars, plus all the upkeep for yourself and for her.
And this was a lot of money, a lot of money in those days.
I don't remember exactly, but, to me, it didn't mean any.
But I know it was a lot of money.
[SIGHS] And she came home and told us that.
And of course, we were beside ourselves.
I mean, what did she let herself into here, this poor woman?
And so my mother says, well what did you tell him?
What did you say?
She said, what did I tell him?
How can you even ask me a question like this?
What did I tell him?
I told him, how dare he suggest a thing like this to me?
So he said, well, you-- you've got two people there.
You've got this little girl.
So why can't you, you know?
So I said to him, yes, I do.
But I don't do it for money.
You want to pay me for it, not me.
If I'm caught with this little girl, here, if we're caught, then the last second of my life, I don't want to think I'm going to die because I made money.
I want to think I'm going to die because I wanted to help this child and her mother.
That's how it was.
She brought this badge, R, for my mother.
And my mother went back to the ghetto.
And I stayed behind.
And I'm now going to shorten this thing, because it was very difficult for me to let my mother go on her own.
And my mother was quite pleased to leave me in a safe place, because this was as safe a place as you could ever find.
But for me, to-- to see her go was terrible and to the ghetto and-- and no communication with the ghetto, whatsoever.
And, on the assumption, that she will be coming every Saturday evening, stay over Sunday, and Monday morning, in the wee hours, she'll leave about 4 o'clock in the morning.

She would have to go through the entire town, from one end to the town to the-- to the other, to the ghetto, to join some working group and walk through the town, without armband, without that thing on, join a group, put this on, all of it, and into the ghetto, so that she may, from the ghetto, go out to work again.
I mean, can you visualize?
And there was no way, through the whole week, that I knew whether my mother was alive or not, whether my grandparents were alive or not.
I knew nothing.
And I couldn't bear being there, in this safe place, on my own, most of the day, because she was at work.
But that was neither here nor there, because I didn't feel like talking with her.
And anyway, I didn't speak German.
I suddenly spoke it, out of the blue.
But I didn't speak it there and then yet.
And all I wanted was to go-- to go to the ghetto.
I was locked in.
She locked me.
And I had no way of getting out.
I was totally, totally unhappy.
And my mother was coming on Saturday.
Again, the worry, did she manage to get through the town, taking off the armband, taking this off, detaching.
And that was stitched on.
So she had to put something on top of herself.
It wasn't to take it off.
She had to put another-- a shawl or something over it.
And to walk through the town, A, nobody would recognize her, somebody who might have known her before, should not recognize her now.

B, to be able to get out of the ghetto, with some group, detach you-- herself and not be spotted by anyone, that she does that, and walk all through the town, walking, walking, walking to come here, in order to sit all through Sunday and so forth, huh?
And Sunday, at 4 o'clock in the morning, leave in the dark to go through the whole town, where there is a curfew.
One is not allowed to.
She did it.
So-- I mean, how can I relate this, to give a-- a-- a picture of what it really was like?
It was-- it was an unbelievable situation, even though I was in-- in-- in a very safe, or so it would have appeared, haven for those days.
So at one point, I said to Frau Wieth, I'm not staying.
I-- I don't.
I really-- I appreciate, but I'm-- I want-- I'm going back to the ghetto.
I was throwing-- sorry.
There were groups, Jewish groups walking to work, going to work and passing our windows.
We were on the third floor.
Occasionally, I'd roll something, you know, throw down to a group and something.
And it would be, please, see if my mother is alive.
You know, I mean it was all very dangerous what we did.
It was absolutely stupid, almost.
But I was so desperate.
And these people would, whoever, pick-- pick something like this up and find my mother and deliver.
And she would bring, and that person would then leave a message somewhere, where I would see.
I mean, you-- you know, somewhere where I would see a message would be left under the house, under a s stone, under some-- or make a-- make a sign, all is well, you know?
That would be it.
That's how I knew.
And one day, Frau Wieth comes home and says, look, you won't be alone anymore.
I've got somebody for you.
So I go, oh, my God, what has she done now?
Because, in those days, you know, I really was smarter than she was, because she was not-- what should I say?
On the same-- on the same planet as I was there.
Oh, what have you done?
Who-- what do you mean, you've got somebody for me?
Where from?
Who is she?
So she says, you know this chemist, what I get my medications from, you know, Mr.
Podoshin. ?] You know, I-- I went to him, and I asked him if he doesn't know of anybody that I could possibly take into hiding, because I have you here.
Can you imagine?
So he thought it was some kind of a trap, that she wants to trap him into something.
He couldn't believe anybody in their-- in their right senses, you know, a German person, coming and saying a thing like this and asking-- asking, have you got somebody I'll take into hiding?
So he said, no.
I haven't got anyone, of course not.
My wife is in the ghetto and I-- with my son.
I haven't gone anybody.
You know, thank you.
That's kind of you.
That was it.
So she went, time and time again.
About the fourth time when she asked him, he really thought that she must mean it.
And she told him the kind of a girl that she has with her.
And that this girl is terribly unhappy.
She told him the whole story.
Because her mother is in the ghetto now.
And she needs a companion for this girl.
So he said, you know, my wife, she's right here, under the floorboards, here, under the shop, under the chemist dispensary.
If you take her-- will you?
She said, yes, of course.
I keep asking you all the time.
Of course, I'll take her.
So he couldn't believe his luck.
He said, we-- don't worry about the food.
I'll provide the food.
I'll send somebody with it.
She said don't-- don't talk to me about anything like this.
I just want her up there.
She was sharing her food rations with me.
Because everybody had rations.
And the Germans, also, but their rations were not to compare with the other ones.
However, they were not all that exuberant.
But she was sharing them with me.
And he said, I'll send food.
But she said, I don't want to hear anything like this.
Don't talk to me about what you will give me and what you will send for me.
The following-- so she came home and told me this story.
I thought, I'll collapse.
I thought, how could you say-- you know, you don't know this man.
You don't know who-- who are you bringing here.
How can you cope?
I mean, this is for your sake not a good idea.
Oh, yes, yes, it's very good.
And you'll have very nice company.
I'm sure she's a nice lady, an intelligent lady.
She must be.
You'll have good company, don't worry.
And the following day, she brought her.
She brought her.
A very nice lady, she was, too.
And there she was.
Within one week, he came, without even asking.
He came.
We were three.
[DRINKS] And there was a man who was delivering food to them.
She didn't know about it, because it was extremely dangerous to do.
But we couldn't-- they couldn't exist without it.
He was bringing-- he was-- this man, who was paid by them, very, very generously.
And I was going down shopping.
Now, I was going down, because, by that time, I spoke German absolutely fluently.
I spoke German, by that time, like she does.
I-- having spoken with her or being with her, that's how I learned.
Equally, I learned, because she had a library of books there, which, when I was on my own, that was the only thing I could occupy my mind with.
I would take the books and started deciphering the Gothic writing, which I had no idea what it was.
But I saw, say, a picture of Napoleon, so I-- I wanted-- so I saw what was written under that.
So I knew this was an N.
This was an A.
This was-- you know?
And this is how I learned to read and to write and to speak German.
And I then spoke German, utterly, utterly fluently.
And I could easily pass for a German child.
So my mother made a sort of Germanic-looking dirndl for me, dress.
And I had my braids.
And even though I was dark, it didn't matter.
She was also dark, Frau Wieth.
It didn't matter.
To the Germans, that was not important.
It was very important to the local population.
When you were dark, you were automatically-- but not to the Germans.
And I was going down shopping.
I had no paper.
I had no documents, nothing whatsoever.
It was very dangerous.
But I was in the SS and polizei district, which is SS and police quarter.
And all the shops were only for Germans, only for Germans.
However, who was buying, who was shopping?
Not the Germans.
The Germans had maids, had cleaners, had servants, who were the locals.
They were Polish or Ukrainians.
And they were doing the shopping for them.
And there was I.
I would come.

And I, when I looked at the queue-- there was always a queue-- I didn't think it was a wise idea to put myself in a queue and expose myself at the time stretch, for half an hour or 15 minutes or however long it would take to stand and be visible.
So I decided I should take-- she had a fold-- a sort of folding frame, photographic frame, a leather folded frame, which was standing there.
And in it were two photographs of her ex-boyfriend, the Gestapo man, in his absolute, full regalia, top to bottom, you know-- I mean.
One was with the hat on, with his, you know, skull, that skull on it, black-- black uniform, with all the epaulets and everything there.
And the other one was the uniform, without the hat, without the cap on.
And he looked like a god.
He looked like a Germanic god, what Hitler actually wanted the race to look like, this is what he looked like.
Blond, tall, blue-eyed, mane of blond hair, absolutely one can say gorgeous to look at if it weren't for this horrid uniform on top.
And I used to take this with me as my document, because I thought, well, so long as I seem all right, nothing, I'm fine.
But when I feel-- and feeling went a long way in those days, you know, and instinct.
You had a sixth sense, no doubt, about that.
You knew when somebody was looking at you from the back.
And sort of, you felt it.
When I feel a little bit uncomfortable, I unfold this thing, very visibly, ostentatiously almost.
And take out some money from there or whatever from there.
And when anybody sees this, that's that.
And that was my document.
I'm only sorry I haven't kept it.
I haven't got any sign, any-- any trace of this, because that was truly, on many occasions, my document.
And on a number of occasions, I would come to shop.
I never went into the queue.

But I must say, with the-- the pure Germanic attitude that they had, you know, their-- their chutzpah, if I might call it that, I would go immediately to the top and demand to be served, which was a terrible thing to do.
But I said, I have no time to wait here in queues and so on.
And the people, who were waiting, were not German, so they were underdogs.
They could wait, in other words.
I am not the one to wait.
I haven't got the time to wait.
Please, this and this and this.
This is what I need.
And off I went, because I was afraid to stand there.
And I would produce my thing.
And then I heard, not on one occasion, look at her, look at that.
If she weren't German, you'd-- you would know she is a Jew.
Only because I was dark.
I heard that not on one occasion.
And it was petrifying.
And I was also frightened that somebody may recognize me.
They might have known me from before, you know?
It's-- it's-- it was all very possible.
So I did this terribly unpleasant and not nice thing by going straight to the front and demanding to be served.
And that's the way we got some food up.
On one such occasion, I was-- there was a very small queue, and they were mostly German women.
About three people in front, so I didn't want to do my bit that way.
I queued, stood there.
And it was nearly my turn.
I produced my thing, because my money was in it, and went to take the money out.
And suddenly, from behind me, I hear, oh, dear God, this is [?
Sepi. ?] And a man embracing me from the back.
And-- and I froze, absolutely froze.
I had no idea who I'm dealing with, what it is.
But this is [?
Sepi ?] was-- was bad, was very bad news.
And the man sort of let's go and looks at me this way.
And-- and says, where is he?
Where is your papi?
Where is he?
And I immediately realized, this must be somebody who knows him very well, because I didn't keep that for such a long time, just literally took the money out.
And where is your papi?
So I mean-- Oh, I said, my papi, my papi is on the Eastern Front.
He's not here at all.
I was in a terrible schtuck.
And he said, yes, but so where is-- where is mami?
Where are you all?
I want to see you.
I want to see your mami.
I'm not here for very long. where are you?
Where do you live?
Oh, I said, well, I don't live very far from here, really very near, almost around the corner.
So when you've done your shopping, I'll take you.
We'll go home.
But I said, I don't know you.
Who are you?
So he gave me some name.
Oh, you don't know.
You don't remember me, because when I last saw you, you were a little, little girl.
I haven't seen your papi in four years or five years or something, you know.
Can you believe this?
So all right.
I said, yes, of course, I will.
I did my shopping.
And he was immediately behind me.
I said, I'll wait for you outside.
When you've done your shopping, we go.
I went outside.
And you can well believe how I ran.
Let me tell you, that if I were to run the marathon today, it would be nothing.
It would be nothing to compare to the way I put my two legs to that ground and ran.
